data.title: Market Voices: Trump on Fed nominee, obesity drug warning, Dow layoffs
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks. 

  • President Trump said he plans to announce who he will nominate for Federal Reserve chair next week.

“We’re going to be announcing next week,” Trump said during a Cabinet meeting Thursday, according to Bloomberg. “We’re going to be announcing the head of the Fed, who that will be, and it will be a person that will, I think, do a good job.”

Leading candidates for the position are said to include National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett, Fed Governor Christopher Waller, former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh, and BlackRock (BLK) Senior Managing Director Rick Rieder. Current Fed Chair Jerome Powell is slated to step down from the job in May. 

  • British regulators are updating product labeling for GLP-1 and GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist drugs, which include the hugely popular weight-loss medications Wegovy and Zepbound, to reflect the potential risk of users developing life-threatening pancreatic conditions. 

The labels will be updated "to highlight the potential risk of severe acute pancreatitis with these products, including rare reports of necrotizing and fatal pancreatitis," according to the U.K.'s MHRA.

Drugs that will carry the warning include dulaglutide, exenatide, liraglutide, semaglutide, and tirzepatide. Novo Nordisk (NVO) markets semaglutide under the brand names Wegovy and Ozempic, while Eli Lilly (LLY) sells tirzepatide under the names Zepbound and Mounjaro. 

  • Dow (DOW) said it plans to eliminate around 4,500 jobs under a new restructuring plan called Transform to Outperform. 

"The goal of Transform to Outperform is to achieve significant growth and productivity gains that elevate Dow's competitive position," said Dow COO Karen Carter in a statement. 

"We are building on the momentum of our current self-help measures—transforming Dow into a company that is more resilient, consistently delivers growth, enables customer success, and delivers greater shareholder value across the cycle," Carter added.

Earlier Thursday, Dow reported a widened year-over-year Q4 loss on lower sales. The chemicals company employs around 34,600 people worldwide.

data.title: Trump says Eli Lilly to build six “big” U.S. plants
data.description:
• Citing a conversation with Eli Lilly (LLY) CEO David Ricks, President Donald Trump said on Thursday that the weight loss drugmaker plans to build six “big” manufacturing plants in the U.S.
  • "I spoke with the head of Eli Lilly, who's a fantastic guy, a star actually—he's a star, very smart—and he told me he's building six plants in the United States, big ones," Trump said during a cabinet meeting at the White House.
  • The Indiana-based pharma giant pledged to begin construction work on four domestic manufacturing sites last year as part of a plan to more than double its recent investments in domestic medicine production to over $50B.
  • The company has already named Alabama, Virginia, and Texas as sites for three of those plants.

data.title: York Space Systems jumps after $629M IPO prices above range
data.description:
Shares of York Space Systems (YSS) climbed 7.2% Thursday after the satellite manufacturer raised $629 million in an upsized initial public offering in the U.S. The stock traded as high as  $38.37 by early afternoon, above the $34 IPO price.

The space and defense company sold 18.5 million shares, increasing the size of the deal from an initial plan to offer 16 million shares at a range of $30 to $34. Based on the number of shares outstanding disclosed in regulatory filings, the offering values the company at about $4.65 billion.

York’s (YSS) market debut comes amid heightened investor interest in defense-related companies and ahead of a widely anticipated public listing by SpaceX (SPACE), which is expected to be one of the largest IPOs on record. That interest has been reinforced by geopolitical developments and renewed pressure from President Donald Trump on U.S. allies to boost defense spending, alongside his proposal to lift the U.S. military budget to $1.5 trillion by 2027.

The company has highlighted its potential role in the proposed Golden Dome missile-defense initiative, a project the White House has estimated could cost $175 billion, though independent analysis suggests the ultimate cost could be significantly higher. York has supplied satellites to the U.S. Space Development Agency’s low-earth-orbit constellation, a program expected to play a role in the Golden Dome architecture.

Founded in 2012, York designs, builds and operates satellites for government and commercial customers. Chief Executive Officer Dirk Wallinger previously held positions at defense contractors including Lockheed Martin (LMT) and General Dynamics (GD) before launching the company.

York’s (YSS) primary customers include the U.S. Air Force and the Space Development Agency, which accounts for most of its revenue and backlog. The company reported having completed 74 missions and accumulated more than four million hours in orbit, according to its filings.

For the nine months ended Sept. 30, York (YSS) posted a comprehensive loss of $54.9 million on $280.9 million in revenue, compared with a loss of $73.1 million on $176.9 million in revenue a year earlier.

The IPO is expected to reduce AE Industrial Partners’ economic stake in York (YSS) to about 24%, though the private equity firm will retain voting control. AE Industrial had previously explored a sale of the company at a valuation exceeding $2 billion, according to earlier reports.

data.title: Wendy's eyes expanding its presence in Mexico
data.description:
The Wendy's Company (WEN) plans to increase its presence in Mexico, with a particular focus on Tijuana and the broader Baja California region. The restaurant company has set the region as its next priority development hub and is recruiting an experienced franchise partner to lead that build‑out, with additional white space flagged in Baja California Sur and Sonora. T

Looking ahead, Wendy's (WEN) sees Mexico as a "high‑potential" market that can ultimately support more than 400 restaurants, in comparison to the just over 40 units in operation today. On a broader basis, Wendy's (WEN) is positioning Mexico as a strategic hub for its wider Latin American growth plans.

To attract prospective operators, Wendy's (WEN) is hosting a Wendy’s Day franchise recruiting event in San Diego on February 10, offering interviews, tours, and brand education, and is directing candidates to its international franchising site. The company has also made recent investments in local marketing, a national marketing fund, supply chain capabilities, and technology upgrades to support rapid new‑unit growth and digital ordering across Mexico.

Wendy's (WEN) history in Mexico dates back roughly three decades, with expansion beginning in the late 1980s as part of the chain’s early international push. By 2001, Tijuana hosted Wendy’s 6,000th global restaurant, operated by franchisee Wenco Mexico, underscoring the border region’s longstanding strategic role. The last update from Wendy's (WEN) on Mexico indicates that it had more than 40 restaurants in the nation.

data.title: Tractor Supply outlines 4%–6% sales growth target for 2026 while expanding store and delivery initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tractor Supply Company (TSCO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Harry Lawton stated that "our fourth quarter results came in below our expectations. Results reflected a shift in consumer spending with essential categories remaining resilient while discretionary demand moderated and emergency response was absent versus last year." Lawton identified three primary drivers: cycling last year's hurricane recovery, big-ticket categories experiencing a step down, and underperformance in holiday and seasonal categories due to a highly promotional environment and softer demand. He emphasized that "customer engagement remained healthy throughout the quarter and our consumable, usable and edible categories continue to perform very well, reinforcing the resilience of our needs-based model."
  • Lawton highlighted a full year net sales increase of 4.3% to $15.5 billion, comparable store sales up 1.2%, and digital business delivering high single-digit growth. He noted strong customer retention, active customer counts, and store expansion with 99 new stores opened in 2025.
  • Lawton explained ongoing investments in technology, including expanded use of AI and a growing relationship with OpenAI to improve forecasting, inventory flow, and productivity. He pointed to advances in direct sales and final-mile delivery, stating "we increased capacity and execution, expanding to more than 210 delivery centers covering nearly 25% of our store base."
  • Lawton described Allivet's integration as progressing, with the business delivering approximately $100 million in sales for the year. He also noted growth in private label and exclusive brands, and plans for continued expansion in 2026.
  • CFO Kurt Barton reported "fourth quarter gross margin declined approximately 10 basis points year-over-year as ongoing cost management was offset by incremental tariffs, elevated promotional activity and higher delivery-related transportation costs." Barton added, "our expense management was a strong point for the quarter. SG&A inclusive of D&A expense increased 6% over the prior year, with nearly 2/3 of the growth rate attributed to new stores and the acquisition of Allivet."

Outlook

  • Barton stated, "for 2026, we expect total sales growth in the range of 4% to 6%, driven by continued new store openings and improving comparable store sales. We expect comp sales growth of 1% to 3%."
  • He projected operating margin in the range of 9.3% to 9.6%, a diluted EPS range of $2.13 to $2.23, and plans for 100 new store openings. Barton added, "we plan to open 100 new stores that are low-risk, high-return organic growth opportunities."
  • Barton noted expectations for "continued expansion driven by ongoing cost management initiatives, growth in our exclusive brands, retail media and continued supply chain efficiencies," but acknowledged partial offsets from delivery costs and tariffs.
  • The company expects "share repurchases between $375 million and $450 million, representing approximately 1% to 1.5% of shares outstanding."
  • Barton explained that guidance assumes a more balanced comp sales performance across all quarters and a more normalized investment environment compared to prior years.

Financial Results

  • Lawton reported Q4 net sales increased 3.3% to $3.9 billion, with comparable store sales up 0.3%. Fourth quarter diluted EPS was $0.43. The digital business delivered high single-digit growth.
  • The company posted positive comps in 11 of 15 regions, with declines concentrated in the South Atlantic, attributed to the absence of storm activity.
  • Consumable, usable and edible categories delivered low mid-single-digit comparable growth, led by livestock, equine, poultry, and wildlife supplies. Winter seasonal categories posted modest growth, while big-ticket and emergency response categories declined high single digits.
  • Gross margin for the full year expanded 16 basis points, while SG&A including D&A increased to 27.5% of sales. Operating income declined 6.5% year-over-year for Q4.
  • Average store inventory was up approximately 5%, with about one third attributed to tariffs and the remainder to support in-stock levels.

Q&A

  • Steven Zaccone, Citigroup: Asked about gross margin expansion and promotional activity. Barton responded, "the fundamentals of our gross margin initiatives are still very solid... Our gross margin initiatives, our cost management is still producing a strong opportunity for gross margin expansion, albeit modest particularly in the first half of the year."
  • Jonathan Matuszewski, Jefferies: Sought clarity on Q1 trends and weather impact. Lawton said, "we are tracking at, above our plan for the quarter-to-date. But as you said, there's still a lot of sales left to go."
  • Robert Griffin, Raymond James: Queried discretionary weakness. Lawton described the Q4 step down as "specific to Q4, using the word transitory," and highlighted optimism about big-ticket categories for spring.
  • Katharine McShane, Goldman Sachs: Asked about tariff cost cadence. Barton said, "there's not that much variation to what we saw in the second half of 2025... We've been able to offset that through great cost management initiatives."
  • Michael Lasser, UBS: Asked about comp growth return to 3%–5%. Lawton responded, "we remain very committed to our long-term algorithm on our comp sales."
  • Robert Ohmes, BofA Securities: Inquired about direct sales profitability. John Ordus, Chief Stores Officer, stated, "we'll double our specialist count... and we're targeting around $50 million in sales in 2026."
  • Peter Benedict, Baird: Sought details on inflation and Garden Centers. Estep, Chief Merchandising Officer, projected "a little bit of inflation, that kind of potentially at 1% to 2% range from an AUR perspective."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on gross margin sustainability, discretionary trends, and the impact of tariffs, showing a slightly negative to neutral tone, particularly around promotional risks and category underperformance.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing resilience, strategic progress, and cost controls. In Q&A, management frequently used phrases such as "we feel very good" and "we are tracking at, above our plan," signaling continued optimism despite short-term headwinds.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management expressed slightly more caution in light of Q4 underperformance, but management's tone remained focused on normalization and long-term growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 results were below expectations, with softer discretionary demand and the absence of emergency response sales, compared to Q3's strong transaction and comp growth.
  • Guidance for 2026 projects normalization, with more balanced investments and growth expectations, versus a narrower and more optimistic outlook in Q3 for the second half of 2025 and 2026 comps.
  • Strategic focus shifted from heavy investment to self-funding growth initiatives, while analysts in both quarters remained focused on gross margin, tariffs, and consumer behavior.
  • Management's confidence in new store productivity, direct sales, and digital initiatives remained consistent, but Q4 remarks reflected greater emphasis on navigating uncertainty.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management called out the absence of storm activity as a 100 basis point headwind to comps and cited ongoing uncertainty in the broader environment, including "mixed signals" in consumer sentiment and affordability debates.
  • Elevated promotional activity and higher delivery-related transportation costs impacted margins.
  • Tariff pressures are expected to persist at similar levels in the first half of 2026, though management expects to mitigate these with cost controls and selective pricing.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of promotional intensity, the pace of normalization, and whether the core business can deliver comp growth without incremental initiatives.

Final Takeaway

Tractor Supply is navigating a shift in consumer demand and external headwinds but remains committed to normalized growth in 2026, targeting 4% to 6% total sales growth, 1% to 3% comp growth, and a robust expansion of both new stores and last-mile delivery capabilities. Management maintains confidence in the resilience of its needs-based model, ongoing initiatives in technology and direct sales, and its ability to deliver long-term shareholder value through disciplined execution and continued market share gains.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: STMicroelectronics targets $1B data center revenue by 2030 as inventory normalizes and automotive recovery anticipated
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: STMicroelectronics N.V. (STM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jean-Marc Chery, Chairman of the Managing Board, President & CEO, stated that STMicroelectronics delivered revenues of $3.33 billion for Q4 2025, above the midpoint of guidance, with growth driven by Personal Electronics and Communication Equipment, while Automotive was below expectations. Gross margin was 35.2%, also above the midpoint, attributed to a better product mix. Chery highlighted that, "Q4 revenue marked the return to year-over-year growth. During the quarter, we further worked down inventories, both in our balance sheet and in distribution, and we generated a positive $257 million free cash flow."
  • Chery noted, "In Automotive, during the quarter, we grew revenues 3% sequentially. Year-over-year revenues declined, but with continued improvement in the trend," with design wins in both electric and traditional vehicle domains. The NXP MEMS sensor business acquisition is expected to close in H1 2026.
  • In Industrial, Chery reported a 5% sequential and year-over-year increase in revenues, with inventories in distribution normalizing. The STM32 Summit in November showcased innovations including the first microcontroller on an 18-nanometer process and new edge AI software tools.
  • Personal Electronics revenues were above expectations, with Chery confirming, "we strengthened our position in mobile platform and connected consumer devices."
  • In Communication Equipment and Computer Peripherals, revenues rose 23% sequentially, with design wins in silicon carbide-based power solutions and ongoing expansion in optical connectivity for data centers.
  • Lorenzo Grandi, President & CFO, stated, "Gross margin was 35.2%, decreasing 250 basis points year-over-year, mainly due to lower manufacturing efficiencies and to a lesser extent, negative currency effect and lower level of capacity reservation fees."

Outlook

  • Chery reported, "We are expecting Q1 '26 revenues at $3.04 billion, a decrease of 8.7% sequentially, plus or minus 350 basis points. We expect our gross margin to be about 33.7%, plus or minus 200 basis points, including about 220 basis points of unused capacity charges."
  • Net CapEx for 2026 is planned at about $2.2 billion to support capacity additions for cloud optical interconnect and the manufacturing reshaping plan.
  • Chery emphasized, "We are entering '26 with a better visibility than entering '25 with the inventory correction in distribution progressively improving."
  • Management projects that in silicon carbide power devices, after a contraction in 2025, revenues are expected to recover to 2024 levels by 2027. For data centers, they aim to "deliver $1 billion revenue before 2030 with already USD 500 million in 2026."

Financial Results

  • Grandi reported that Analog products, MEMS, and sensor grew 7.5% year over year, Power and Discrete products decreased 31.6%, Embedded Processing revenues were up 1% to 2%, and RF and optical communication grew 22.9%.
  • By end market, Communication Equipment and Computer Peripherals and Personal Electronics both grew by about 17%, Industrial by about 5%, while Automotive decreased by about 15% year over year.
  • Gross profit in Q4 was $1.17 billion, with total net operating expenses at $906 million, and operating income at $125 million (including $141 million for impairment and restructuring charges). Q4 net loss was $30 million, with diluted earnings per share at (-$0.03). Excluding nonrecurring items, non-U.S. GAAP diluted earnings per share was $0.11.
  • Full-year 2025 net revenues were $11.8 billion, gross margin 33.9%, and net income $166 million. Free cash flow for the year was $265 million.
  • Inventory at year-end was $3.14 billion, with days sales of inventory at 130 days. Net financial position at year-end was $2.79 billion.

Q&A

  • Francois-Xavier Bouvignies, UBS: Asked about the trajectory for above-seasonal trends and cycle recovery. Chery responded, "we are confident in our ability to grow organically for next year... we see for the company some specific growth driver," including automotive, industrial, personal electronics, data centers, and low earth orbit satellites.
  • Bouvignies, UBS: Queried gross margin recovery potential. Grandi answered, "the gross margin today that we see in Q1, we believe is clearly the lowest point in the year, this expectation of 33.7%. So we will see some increase... driven by the seasonality of the revenues in the second half of the year."
  • Andrew Gardiner, Citi: Questioned automotive market recovery and inventory levels. Chery explained, "Inventory correction is certainly gone, but there is a kind of a softness of this kind of application," with future growth expected in electronic architectures, ADAS, and powertrain electronics. Regarding China, Chery noted the ramp-up of the Sanan partnership as a competitive factor.
  • Joshua Buchalter, TD Cowen: Asked about Personal Electronics segment resilience and silicon content. Chery confirmed, "we expect to keep growing in personal electronics driven by our main customer in 2026, thanks to our increased device based on silicon."
  • Stephane Houri, ODDO BHF: Inquired about seasonality and customer programs. Chery replied, "by end of Q2, we believe we will be hold the excess of inventory."
  • Domenico Ghilotti, Equita: Sought clarification on unused charges and second client in low earth orbit. Grandi stated, "unused charges are declining in the first quarter... this trend will continue. Unused capacity will not disappear in the year, but will significantly reduce in the year."
  • Sandeep Deshpande, JPMorgan: Asked about fab loading and product mix impact on gross margin. Grandi explained the impact is related to manufacturing capacity adjustments and shifting production.
  • Sébastien Sztabowicz, Kepler Cheuvreux: Asked about progress in transformation program and OpEx trend. Grandi assured, "we are on track in respect to what we have communicated previously," and expects OpEx to see a low single-digit increase in 2026.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were inquisitive and at times pressed for details on margin recovery, seasonality, and automotive market stabilization, reflecting a neutral to slightly negative tone, especially regarding gross margin and inventory normalization.
  • Management maintained confidence, frequently reiterating growth drivers and operational improvements. Chery used phrases such as "we are confident" and "we see for the company some specific growth driver," indicating a positive tone. Grandi maintained, "we will see some increase" in gross margin.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more optimistic, highlighting better visibility and inventory correction progress. Analysts' tone remained cautious but acknowledged improvements in inventory and market trends.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q1 2026 indicates lower sequential revenue and gross margin compared to Q4 2025, but management projects improvement throughout the year, especially in the second half.
  • The previous quarter focused on inventory correction and margin pressure; the current quarter emphasizes normalization and return to year-over-year growth.
  • Strategic focus has shifted toward leveraging growth drivers in data centers, automotive, and industrial, with renewed confidence in organic growth for 2026.
  • Analysts continue to focus on seasonality, margin sustainability, and market dynamics, while management exhibits greater optimism about cycle recovery and specific market opportunities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing softness in legacy automotive applications and a need to adapt to shifting market mixes and regional dynamics, especially in China.
  • Gross margin pressure from lower manufacturing efficiencies, currency effects, and capacity reservation fee reductions remains a concern, with nonrecurring manufacturing costs set to persist in the near term.
  • Analysts questioned the pace of margin recovery and the stability of demand in core segments.
  • The company continues to monitor and manage risks associated with capacity transitions and inventory normalization.

Final Takeaway

STMicroelectronics ended 2025 with improving revenue dynamics, inventory normalization, and positive free cash flow, entering 2026 with greater visibility and confidence in organic growth. Management projects significant growth opportunities in silicon carbide power devices, data centers, and automotive, supported by innovation and strategic partnerships. While short-term margin pressure and market softness persist in certain areas, the company is positioning itself for recovery and long-term value creation through targeted investments and operational discipline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Group 1 Automotive outlines $640M revenue boost from acquisitions amid UK restructuring and record $3.6B gross profit
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Group 1 Automotive (GPI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Daryl Kenningham highlighted that Group 1 achieved record revenues across all major business lines and record gross profits in parts and service and F&I, emphasizing the company's "relentless focus on operational excellence." He shared, "For the full year, we generated an all-time high gross profit of more than $3.6 billion, including record parts and service gross profit of nearly $1.6 billion."
  • Kenningham reported the sale of 459,000 new and used vehicles in 2025, noting this was another record year. He underscored the acquisition of Lexus and Acura dealerships in Fort Myers, Florida, as well as Mercedes-Benz dealerships in Austin, Texas and Atlanta, Georgia, and three Toyota and one Lexus dealership in the U.K., collectively expected to generate approximately $640 million in annual revenue.
  • He stated, "We disposed of 13 dealerships comprising 32 franchises, which have generated approximately $775 million in annualized revenue. In addition, we repurchased more than 10% of our outstanding shares in 2025."
  • Kenningham addressed ongoing U.K. restructuring, which included a reduction of 537 positions in 2025, consolidation of customer contact centers, and full onshoring of transactional accounting operations. He remarked, "We are seeing positive impact of our U.S. operating practices in the U.K., particularly in aftersales."
  • CFO Daniel McHenry reported, "In the fourth quarter of 2025, Group 1 Automotive reported revenues of $5.6 billion, gross profit of $874 million, adjusted net income of $105 million and adjusted diluted EPS of $8.49 from continuing operations."

Outlook

  • Management described continued focus on operational improvements and cost controls in both the U.S. and U.K. Kenningham explained, "We believe this focus on controlling what we can control from inventory and pricing discipline to aftersales performance, capital allocation and costs positions Group 1 to navigate near-term challenges while continuing to build a stronger, more resilient platform for the long term."
  • McHenry shared, "As we roll into 2026, we should see the benefit of those costs that have been taken out throughout the year fully baked in for the year in 2026."

Financial Results

  • McHenry detailed U.S. operations, noting a slight decline in new vehicle sales but reported that average selling prices continue to increase, while used vehicle revenues rose approximately 4%. F&I GPUs grew nearly 3% versus the prior year. Aftersales produced increased gross profit with customer pay and warranty revenues up approximately 5% and 11%, respectively.
  • SG&A as a percent of gross profit increased 200 basis points sequentially to 67.8%, driven by higher employee expenses. McHenry noted, "We continue to focus heavily on resource management and technology investments to try to maintain SG&A as a percent of gross profit below pre-COVID levels."
  • In the U.K., same-store revenues grew across almost every business line, but new vehicle same-store volumes declined 8.2%. Used vehicle same-store revenues increased over 9% in local currency, although same-store GPUs declined almost 19%. Customer pay revenue rose 9% year-over-year.
  • Liquidity at year-end was $883 million, comprised of $537 million in accessible cash and $346 million available to borrow on the acquisition line. Rent-adjusted leverage was 3.1x. Cash flow generation for the year was $699 million in adjusted operating cash flow and $494 million in free cash flow after $205 million in CapEx. Share repurchases totaled $555 million for 1.3 million shares, plus additional repurchases in Q1 2026.

Q&A

  • Rajat Gupta, JPMorgan: Sought details on impairments for the quarter. McHenry clarified, "The impairments related virtually totally to the U.S. business... The principal brand, I guess, that we had impairments within was within the Audi brand."
  • Gupta followed up on productivity actions in the U.S. Kenningham responded, "We are using AI in every part of our business, both customer interface as well as in our back office... we're seeing tangible results, which is resulting in less turnover and more productivity."
  • Patrick Buckley, Jefferies: Asked about the U.K. restructuring timeline. Kenningham stated, "We're in the earlier innings, not the later innings."
  • Buckley further inquired about Chinese OEM penetration in the U.K. Kenningham observed, "Chinese OEMs are the Q4 share leveled off at around a little under 12%. They had a big spike from Q4 '24 to Q4 '25, it leveled off a bit."
  • John Saager, Evercore ISI: Asked about anticipated restructuring magnitude. McHenry said, "I don't see it being anything like that this quarter or this year, 2026. We've done significant work."
  • Saager also asked about used GPUs and SG&A as percent of GP. Kenningham said the company expects upside in U.K. GPUs and targets SG&A at 80% in the U.K. and mid- to high 60% in the U.S. annually.
  • David Whiston, Morningstar: Asked about disposals. Kenningham responded, "I think '25 was more of an outlier year... There will be some more of that in '26. But on a long-term basis, it won't be nearly that active."
  • Whiston also questioned capital allocation. McHenry prioritized keeping leverage below 3x and stated, "We really want to grow the company... through acquisition... and buybacks as and when the time is right."
  • Jeffrey Lick, Stephens: Asked about the impact of lease returns on used car supply. Kenningham said, "A good solid controlled source of premium used cars is really great... I hope and expect that, that will help us later in the year."
  • John Babcock, Barclays: Inquired about U.S. used vehicle market and expectation. Pete DeLongchamps responded, "We're certainly bullish on the used car opportunity this coming year... the used car market is continually a great source for our company's revenue and gross profits."
  • Babcock also asked about GPUs. Kenningham explained, "We saw some softening on the luxuries in the fourth quarter, GPUs, and that was affected us more than normal. I would think that we'll see some moderation of that."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for specifics on impairments, restructuring, and productivity, with a slightly negative to neutral tone, especially regarding U.K. macro conditions and cost pressures.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing operational control, cost management, and portfolio optimization. During Q&A, management was transparent but cautious, admitting "more work to do" and that "we're in the earlier innings" of U.K. restructuring.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' sentiment remained skeptical about U.K. challenges, while management's tone shifted slightly more cautious on restructuring but stayed resolutely focused on operational execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language this quarter reflected a more cautious tone on the U.K. environment, with greater emphasis on ongoing restructuring and operational improvement, while the previous quarter stressed progress and optimism about aftersales expansion.
  • The current quarter revealed a continued focus on U.K. cost reduction, headcount streamlining, and portfolio management, compared to last quarter’s initial integration efforts and JLR exit announcement.
  • Analysts repeated questions about restructuring, cost control, and U.K. headwinds, as in the previous quarter.
  • Management’s confidence in U.S. operations remained high, with ongoing discipline in capital allocation and productivity, while showing more measured optimism about U.K. recovery.
  • Key metrics such as revenues, gross profit, and free cash flow were lower in Q4 compared to Q3, while share repurchase activity intensified.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited U.K. macroeconomic headwinds, persistent inflation, increased competition from new entrants (notably Chinese OEMs), and regulatory pressures as ongoing risks.
  • Impairments related to the Audi brand and the Maryland/DC market were reported.
  • The company is executing further restructuring, including potential additional OEM site exits.
  • Analysts voiced concerns about the pace and effectiveness of U.K. restructuring, SG&A management, and the potential impact of lease returns and market normalization on margins.

Final Takeaway

Group 1 Automotive's 2025 close featured record performance in gross profit and vehicle sales, supported by disciplined capital deployment, significant acquisitions expected to add $640 million in annual revenue, and intensified share repurchases. The company signaled a proactive approach to restructuring in the U.K., cost management, and technology-driven productivity, while remaining focused on leveraging its diversified business model to address market uncertainties and drive long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Coterra-Devon merger said to be in advanced talks - FT
data.description:
Coterra Energy (CTRA) +2.5% and Devon Energy (DVN) +1.4% after Financial Times reported the two companies are nearing a deal to merge in what would be the largest oil and gas deal in the U.S. shale industry in nearly two years.

Talks are at an advanced stage, and a deal that would create a company with an enterprise
value of ~$57B could come together as soon as early next week, according to the report.

Both companies own numerous assets in the Permian Basin, and combining their operations would allow them to compete against larger rivals at a time when low crude oil prices are straining many companies in the shale sector.

Bloomberg first reported two weeks ago that the companies were pursuing a deal; shares in Coterra (CTRA) and Devon (DVN) have gained 12% and 7%, respectively, since the first report of merger talks.

data.title: Belgian biopharma Agomab to raise up to $210M in U.S. IPO
data.description:
Agomab Therapeutics (AGMB), a Belgian biopharma focused on immunology and inflammatory diseases, filed to offer 12.5M American Depositary ‌Shares in its U.S. IPO under the ticker symbol "AGMB" on Thursday, seeking to raise up to $210M in net proceeds.

The Antwerp-based company could command a valuation of $828.5M at the top of its marketed range of $15.00 - $17.00 per ADS, assuming roughly 48.7M shares are outstanding immediately after the offering.

Agomab (AGMB) could raise roughly $182M (or $210M if the underwriters fully exercise their option to purchase additional ADSs) if it prices the offering at the midpoint of the range.

The company’s pipeline is led by ontunisertib, a small-molecule drug targeted at Fibrostenosing Crohn’s disease. A severe complication of the inflammatory bowel disorder, Crohn’s disease, FCD has no FDA-approved therapies.  

As for near-term catalysts, Agomab (AGMB) expects to begin a Phase 2b trial for ontunisertib in patients with symptomatic FSCD in H2 2026 based on data from its Phase 2a STENOVA trial, where Part A achieved its primary endpoint.

data.title: L3Harris shares slide on disappointing earnings outlook; fed shutdown hit Q4
data.description:
Shares of L3Harris Technologies (LHX) slid as much as 2.9% Thursday after the company reported disappointing quarterly revenue and issued weaker-than-expected 2026 earnings guidance.

Management forecast full-year 2026 adjusted earnings of $11.30 to $11.50 a share, below the consensus estimate of $12.44.

Fourth-quarter revenue rose 2% from a year earlier to $5.6 billion, but below the consensus estimate of $5.76 billion. The company cited delayed contract awards tied to the late-year government shutdown, which weighed on reported sales, particularly in its space and airborne systems business.

L3Harris (LHX) supplies systems for U.S. and allied defense platforms, including electronic warfare, missile warning sensors, secure communications, avionics and space-based surveillance capabilities. Its technology is embedded in aircraft, missile defense systems, naval vessels and satellites.

Earnings beat driven by margins and cost actions

The company posted adjusted earnings of $2.86 a share, topping the Wall Street consensus estimate of $2.76 a share. Adjusted segment operating margin rose to 15.7% from 15.3% a year earlier, reflecting cost savings and portfolio actions.

Net income fell to about $299 million, or $1.59 a share, from about $453 million, or $2.37 a share, a year earlier, as lower operating income and higher unallocated corporate costs weighed on results.

Fourth-quarter operating margin declined to 7.0% from 10.3% in the prior year, driven by higher corporate expenses and weaker performance at Aerojet Rocketdyne following a non-cash impairment charge.

Government shutdown pressures results

Revenue growth was modest across most segments, with the company noting that delayed awards tied to the government shutdown pushed expected sales out of the quarter. L3Harris (LHX) said those delays affected both fourth-quarter and full-year revenue, primarily in space and airborne systems.

Operating income for the quarter fell to $393 million from $569 million a year earlier, while segment operating income declined slightly year over year.

CEO comments and outlook

“2025 was a clear inflection point for L3Harris,” Chief Executive Christopher Kubasik said in a statement, citing record orders and improved cash flow, while acknowledging the impact of delayed customer awards late in the year.

Looking ahead, L3Harris (LHX) forecast 2026 revenue of $23.0 billion to $23.5 billion, in line with the consensus estimate of $23.3 billion.
